title: Military Convoy Explosion
instructions:
It's around noon on a hot day in April.  You are in one of the middle trucks on a convoy in the middle of the desert.
You tighten the muscles in your face and forehead. Your eyes, scan the area, looking for any signs of danger. You hear an explosion from up the road.
Your heart beats faster. You hear the loud blast, then a ringing in your ears. You are breathing faster. You see the lead truck pull sideways and stop.
You tense the muscles in your jaws and back. You see your medic run up the road to help out. You think: “Oh my God, they're all dead”. You feel hot all over.
You squint your eyes and look for your friend in the turret. But all you see is smoke and dust. Both your commanders are yelling for you to keep your head up.
Now you have to cover the field on both sides. You feel jittery and shaky all over. Your whole body is shaking. We're supposed to leave the area under all
this gunfire. Your stomach is in a knot. Your whole body is tense. Everyone's confused and there's no leadership. There's a sinking feeling in your chest.
You feel trapped. Suddenly, you realize you haven't been paying attention to your sector. You look around frantically for the enemy. Your heart is pounding.
Your hands clench the weapon tightly. You accidentally pulled the trigger and send out a couple of rounds. Sweat is pouring off your forehead.
You feel choked up. You feel helpless and alone. You think about your wife and kids. You breathe in smoke from the explosive.
You feel empty, drained, and hollow. Tears come to your eyes.

title: Coastal Storm Flooding
instructions:
It's late afternoon on a humid day in August. You're in a small wooden house near the coast. The wind outside is howling, stronger than you've ever heard before.
Your fingers dig into the sides of the couch, clenching tight. The windows shake violently, giving you glimpses of the furious storm outside. You hear a deafening crash — a tree has fallen nearby.
Your heart races, adrenaline surging through you. The floor beneath you vibrates, and a torrential rain pours outside, drumming hard on the roof. You try to breathe deeply, but your chest feels tight.
You think: \"This is it, the house will be swept away\". Water starts seeping in, first a trickle, then a flood, cold against your feet. The power's out, and with every gust of wind, the structure creaks and groans.
You move to the center of the room, away from the windows, but feel so exposed. The pit in your stomach grows. The walls of the house shudder.
The sound is deafening. You close your eyes and think of your family, hoping they're safe. Suddenly, there's a loud crash, and water rushes in faster. 
It's up to your ankles now, cold and relentless. You scramble onto the couch, trying to keep your balance. There's a sinking feeling in your chest. 
You're trapped, and there's no way out. All around you, the storm rages on, indifferent to your fear. You feel alone, lost in its fury, wondering if dawn will ever come.

title: Apartment Intruder
instructions:
You're in your downtown apartment, the familiar hum of the city below. Suddenly, there's a loud knock at the door. 
Your muscles tighten, a wave of unease surging through you. You weren't expecting anyone. Another knock, more forceful this time. 
You inch closer, cautiously peeping through the peephole. There's a shadowy figure, unfamiliar and intimidating. 
Your heart races as you back away from the door, but the persistent knocking continues, now accompanied by a muffled voice demanding entry. 
You can feel the weight of dread in your chest. Thoughts race, \"Why are they here? What do they want?\" You grab your phone, contemplating calling for help, but your fingers tremble, making it hard to dial.
The sound of splintering wood sends a shock through your body. They're trying to force their way in. You think of hiding in the bathroom, or maybe the bedroom, but fear paralysis grips you. 
Suddenly, the door gives way with a loud crash. Adrenaline courses through you. The intruder's voice is sharp, cold, and demanding. You feel exposed, vulnerable. 
Every sound magnifies — the creaking floor, your rapid breath, their menacing steps. The distance between you seems to close rapidly. You're cornered.
Thoughts of loved ones flash before your eyes. Memories, dreams, things unsaid. You feel trapped, the walls closing in. The intruder's shadow looms larger, and with it, the palpable threat of violence. 
Powerlessness engulfs you. In the thick of danger, you wonder if anyone will hear, if help will come.

title: Highway Collision
instructions:
It's a crisp evening, and the highway stretches ahead, illuminated only by your car's headlights and the sparse street lamps. The radio plays a soft tune, and you're lost in thought, driving 
at a steady pace. Suddenly, out of the periphery of your vision, you spot a pair of headlights swerving erratically. Before you can even process the situation, they're coming straight for you. Time seems
to slow as you grip the steering wheel tighter, your heart hammering against your chest. Panic courses through you, and instinctively you try to swerve away. The deafening sound of metal crunching 
against metal fills your ears as the two vehicles collide. The force of the impact throws you against your seatbelt, knocking the wind out of you. Your vision blurs from the sudden jolt, and 
shards from the shattered windshield fly everywhere. The screech of tires, the blaring of horns, and the alarm of your own car create a cacophony of chaos. As the initial shock begins to 
fade, you try to move, to check yourself for injuries, but pain shoots through you. The other vehicle, equally mangled, is just a few meters away. Thoughts race, \"Was it my fault?\" 
You fumble for your phone, hands trembling, to call for help. Every sound is magnified - distant sirens, the low murmur of other drivers stopping to assist, your own ragged breathing.
Fear and helplessness wash over you. Memories of loved ones and past journeys flash through your mind. Amid the shattered glass and twisted metal, you wonder if you'll ever feel comfortable behind the wheel again.

title: Desert Ambush
instructions:
In the merciless grip of the midday sun, the desert becomes a vast, glaring oven. Each grain of sand below seems to pulse with latent menace. Your truck, sandwiched in the heart of a convoy,
feels like a metal trap. Every pore on your face tightens, every nerve strung to breaking point. You're hyper-aware, the weight of potential danger causing the very air to thicken around you. 
Without warning, a deafening explosion ruptures the silence, its violent shockwave sending a visceral jolt down your spine. Your heart doesn't just race; it gallops, threatening to leap out 
of your chest. The once-organized procession is now a scene of pandemonium, dominated by a monstrous plume of smoke where the lead truck once was. An icy dread laces your veins with the 
horrifying realization: \"They've been obliterated.\" Everywhere you turn, you're met with a blinding, choking haze. A cacophony of shouts and orders, desperate and filled with panic,
assaults your ears. But they sound distant, as if underwater, drowned out by the screaming alarm in your mind. Every fiber of your being feels electrified, vibrating with a frenzied energy.
Commands echo around you, yet they seem abstract, distant. Your gut churns, twisting in on itself, each second stretching into an agonizing eternity. Amid the turmoil, a paralyzing thought
hits: you've lost track of your position, your duty. Your mind races, thoughts scattering like leaves in a storm. Hands slick with sweat clutch your weapon so hard it's painful. 
Its cold metal feels volatile, ready to explode. The trigger yields beneath your grip, shots firing haphazardly. With every deafening report, the vision of home, of family, 
becomes more distant, more unreachable. The sharp, acrid stench of explosives fills your nostrils, a constant reminder of the looming threat. Waves of nausea, dread, and 
raw vulnerability crash over you, pulling you deeper into a vortex of overwhelming terror.

title: six
instructions:
You're driving on a deserted highway at dusk, rain hammering the windshield like nails. Wipers struggle, streaking the glass, turning the twisting road into a smeared nightmare. Suddenly the engine coughs weakly, lights dim to a sickly yellow, and the car rolls to a silent stop in the middle of absolute nowhere. Phone screen shows endless searching—no signal, just crackling static. Thunder rolls low; lightning reveals flat, empty fields vanishing into gray.
Your pulse spikes as you step out into the freezing downpour, clothes soaked in seconds, fumbling blindly under the hood with shaking hands and no clue what you're looking for. A distant rumble builds—not thunder this time, but the deep growl of an approaching engine. Headlights slice through the rain, slowing deliberately. You wave frantically, but a cold knot twists in your gut: stranger in the storm, no one around for miles.
The truck halts. Door creaks open slowly. A hooded figure steps down, boots splashing. Heart slamming against your ribs, you back away fast, mind racing through every roadside disappearance you've ever heard. They call something—words swallowed by wind and rain. Panic surges; you lunge back to your car, slam the locks down, but the key turns uselessly in the ignition.
The figure closes in, knocks once on the window—slow, deliberate. Face hidden in shadow. Trapped in the cold, dripping metal box, vulnerability crashes over you like ice water. Rain intensifies, drumming louder, washing away any hope of another car passing. Thoughts spiral darker: abducted, dragged into the ditch, left bleeding—no one knows your route tonight.
Knocking turns to heavy pounding. Glass fogs thick from your ragged, panicked breaths. The storm devours sound, sight, everything. No distant headlights. No help. Dread coils tighter as the figure stands motionless now, patient, waiting for your resolve to crack first.

title: seven
instructions:
Late at night in your quiet apartment, a faint scratching begins inside the walls—slow, careful, like fingernails testing wood. You freeze under the covers, convincing yourself it's old pipes or the building settling. It pauses, then starts again, rhythmic and unmistakably closer.
Heart thumping hard, you sit up and flick the lamp—bulb buzzes once and dies, plunging the room into thick darkness that sharpens every tiny sound. A soft thump comes from the closet, heavy enough to make the door rattle slightly. Sweat prickles your forehead as you force yourself to stand, hand trembling on the knob. Faint whispers seep through the wood—low, human, impossible to make out.
Terror surges sharp: someone hiding inside, waiting for you to open it. You yank the door wide—nothing but hanging clothes and a sudden draft of icy air from a vent you never noticed before. Relief flickers, then dies. Footsteps now walk directly overhead, slow and measured, then stop right above your bed. Paranoia floods in: you've been watched for weeks, targeted, completely alone.
You snatch a kitchen knife, knuckles bone-white, breaths coming short and shallow. Scratching resumes—this time under the floorboards, circling. Walls seem to press closer, air thinner. Visions flash: door kicked in, overpowered in silence, screams lost to empty neighbors. Phone sits across the dark room, too far to reach without crossing shadow.
Panic swells to a choking peak; every creak feels like the next move. The night stretches endlessly, isolation breeding deeper, suffocating fear. Dawn might come—or it might only reveal whatever has been listening, patient, all along.

title: eight
instructions:
You're trapped in an aging elevator halfway between floors. Overhead lights hum erratically, flickering like they're about to quit. You mash the alarm button over and over—nothing but dead silence in reply. The car sways gently; thick cables groan and creak high above. Air grows warmer, heavier, already tasting stale and used.
Heart racing, you pound on the doors with both fists; your voice bounces back hollow and small. A sudden lurch drops the car an inch—stomach flips violently. Terror pours in: cables fraying, sudden freefall, metal crushing on impact below.
Sweat soaks through your shirt in seconds. Breaths come too fast, too shallow. Images of family waiting at home flash—oblivious, hours from knowing anything's wrong. The walls feel closer with every flicker, pressing against your chest and mind. You sink to the floor, knees weak, thoughts spinning: slow suffocation from bad air, endless waiting in the dark, or the final sickening drop.
Another loud creak echoes—something shifting, giving. Desperation claws up your throat; you claw at the door seam, nails splitting, fingers raw. Isolation makes every groan louder, every second heavier—no one coming, no signal, no way out. Time blurs into a tight coil of dread.
Will rescuers ever arrive, or will the cables snap while you're still breathing? The uncertainty gnaws deeper, leaving you trembling, small, in the failing dim light.

title: nine
instructions:
You're swimming alone, farther from shore than you realized, as the sun dips low and turns the water gold then black. Gentle waves rock you. Then a wide, dark shadow passes swiftly beneath—too large, too fast. Blood turns to ice; arms and legs lock mid-stroke. The beach looks tiny now, impossibly distant; the current tugs you sideways, farther out.
Heart hammers against your ribs. You tread water desperately, eyes darting down into the darkening depths. Fins slice the surface nearby—slow, lazy circles closing in. Panic rips through you; you thrash toward land, arms burning already, exhaustion sinking teeth in fast. Saltwater stings your eyes; every breath comes ragged and desperate.
Thoughts tumble wildly: jaws closing, dragged under screaming, body lost to the deep forever. Something cold brushes your calf—seaweed or the first graze of teeth? Adrenaline surges, propelling you harder, muscles screaming in protest. Light fades fast; water turns opaque, hiding whatever's below.
No boat in sight, no lifeguard whistle, just endless open sea pressing around you. A larger wave crashes over your head; lungs seize, burning for air. Visions of blood clouding the water, alone in the black abyss. You break surface gasping, coughing—shore barely closer. Fear paralyzes; every small ripple feels like the final approach.
Sun vanishes. Night swallows the horizon. Will you reach sand, or become just another name no one finds? Dread coils deep, the cold black water whispering your end with every swell.

title: ten
instructions:
You wake choking to the smoke alarm shrieking in pitch darkness. Thick, acrid smoke already stings your throat and eyes—fire burning somewhere close. You bolt upright, coughing hard, head spinning with disorientation. The hallway beyond your door glows angry orange; flames lick hungrily at the frame.
Heart slams so hard it hurts. You lunge for the window—handle jams, won't budge. Smoke pours in faster, coiling around you; eyes stream tears, lungs sear with every breath. Panic crashes full force: trapped, burning alive inside the only place that's ever felt safe.
You throw a blanket over your head and shoulders, dash into the blistering heat; skin tightens and stings instantly. Stairs creak and groan underfoot, wood weakening fast. Family asleep downstairs—still unaware, still breathing smoke. A heavy beam crashes somewhere behind, sparks flying, sealing the path back.
Adrenaline drives you forward through the choking haze, but smoke twists your sense of direction. Flames roar louder, devouring walls and memories. No sound of neighbors stirring; your phone's screen glows useless in the heat, battery dying fast.
You collapse against the front door, vision tunneling black at the edges. Desperation surges—you kick the lock repeatedly, foot throbbing with pain. Visions flood: lungs filling with smoke, body charred beyond recognition, found too late. The blaze surges hotter; outside air teases through tiny cracks, mocking you.
Will you force the door in time, or does the fire claim you here? Terror peaks with every shallow, scorching breath you manage to steal.

title: eleven
instructions:
You wake to slow, heavy dripping in the pitch-black bedroom. At first you think bathroom faucet leaking—but the sound is too close, too thick and deliberate. Lying rigid, you watch by faint moonlight as a dark, wet spot blooms on the ceiling directly above your bed, spreading slowly, glistening. A faint metallic smell—copper—drifts down when you dare to breathe deeper.
Heart climbs into your throat, pounding loud enough to drown thought. Phone on the nightstand stays black—dead, though you swear you charged it. Dripping speeds up; now a steady patter. A single warm drop splashes your forehead. You bolt upright, choking on the scream that tries to escape.
Something heavy shuffles in the attic—old boards creak under careful weight. Footsteps drag slowly toward the access hatch right over your bed. Pulse thunders in your ears. You swing legs out, bare feet hitting cold floor, backing toward the door without blinking at the ceiling.
Hatch cord dangles, untouched for years—yet it sways gently now. A low, wet rasp floats down—not quite breathing, not quite words. You slam the bedroom door, twist the lock with numb fingers. Relief flickers—then the soft metallic pop of the hatch latch releasing above.
Wood scrapes. A heavy thump lands inside your room. Dragging footsteps resume, moving down the hallway toward you. You reach the front door—deadbolt jams, always sticks when hands shake. A wet handprint appears on the frosted glass panel, fingers splaying, sliding slowly downward.
You finally wrench it free, burst barefoot into the cold black street. Behind you the door clicks shut by itself. No streetlights anywhere. No cars. No voices. The shape stands motionless in your doorway—tall, wrong, still dripping—head tilted as if listening for your next move.
You run harder, terror clawing your chest, certain it's still there—patient, waiting.

title: twelve
instructions:
Last train home after a long shift—carriage almost deserted. Overhead fluorescents buzz and stutter like they're struggling to stay alive. A hooded man sits across the aisle, face completely shadowed, unmoving since you boarded many stops ago. No phone signal this deep underground. Train jerks to a halt between stations—crackling announcement promises fifteen minutes delay. Minutes stretch to thirty, then forty-five.
Air thickens, grows warmer, recycled too many times. He hasn't shifted once—not even to breathe noticeably. Sweat beads on your neck; you glance sideways—his silhouette seems fractionally closer each time lights flicker. Then total blackout. Three heartbeats of absolute dark.
Lights stutter back: he's facing you now. Hood still up, but lower face visible—no mouth, only smooth, unbroken skin. Stomach plummets through the floor. You rise slowly, legs unsteady, pretending casual stretch toward the connecting door. He stands too—coat hangs oddly, as if something larger shifts underneath.
Train lurches forward, stops harder. Lights fail again. In the black you hear fabric rustling, slow deliberate footsteps on metal floor. A cold, impossibly long hand brushes the back of your neck. You scream, spin—carriage empty when light returns.
Train finally moves, gathering speed. You press flat against the door, eyes darting over every reflection and shadow. At the next station doors hiss open. You sprint onto the platform without looking back.
But as the train pulls away you catch the window glimpse: he's seated exactly where he started, head turned toward you, mouthless face tilted slightly, watching. He wasn't trying to catch you on the train. He was waiting for you to run.

title: thirteen
instructions:
Housesitting your aunt's creaky old Victorian on the town's edge—first night unnaturally quiet after city noise. Floorboards pop on their own; wind rattles shutters. Around 2:17 a.m. a child's light giggle floats down from upstairs. Your aunt lives entirely alone—no children, no visitors. You lie frozen, telling yourself it's a TV next door, a neighbor's kid outside.
Giggle returns—closer now, right on the landing. Small footsteps patter across the hallway, pause outside your door. You hold your breath. Knob turns slowly, clicks against the lock you turned earlier. Silence stretches long. You finally exhale, shaking—then notice your phone and wall clock both stuck at 2:17.
Three polite taps on the door—high up, far above any child's reach. Blood runs cold. A sweet whisper slips through the keyhole: "Can you let me in? It's so cold out here." You slide toward the window—yard empty, moonlit grass still. Voice continues calmly: "I left my doll under the bed. She misses me." You look down—porcelain face stares up from the rug, glass eyes catching moonlight. It wasn't there minutes ago.
Knocking resumes, steady, unhurried. You snatch keys and phone, fling the door open. Hallway empty, lights dim but on. You bolt downstairs—every door in the house slams shut in perfect sequence behind you, final one locking with a sharp click.
On the porch, trembling, you stare back. A small handprint slowly fogs the upstairs bedroom glass—from the inside. You spend the night in your car down the block, engine running, lights on. Morning: house looks ordinary again. Aunt asks casually why one doll is missing from her collection. You never tell her the truth. You never step foot inside again.

title: fourteen
instructions:
Working late alone on the 27th floor—most lights off, only your desk lamp and faint emergency strips glowing. City glitters far below through dark windows. Elevator dings unexpectedly—doors slide open, empty car waiting. Security line rings dead. It stays open, expectant. You ignore it, keep typing.
Ten minutes pass. Another ding—still empty. A faint smell creeps in: wet concrete, mildew, something metallic and wrong. Next ding sounds lower, almost a growl. Doors open: interior light strobes wildly. On the floor—a single large wet barefoot print, water pooling and spreading slowly outward.
Mouth goes dry. You back away toward the stairwell. As you pass, the light steadies for a heartbeat: in the polished metal wall of the elevator, your reflection stands perfectly still while you move—head tilting slowly, watching you.
You run—stairwell door locked from inside, emergency only. Wet slapping footsteps approach from behind, slow and deliberate. You sprint to the copy room at the far end, slam and lock the door, crouch behind the machine, breathing hard through your sleeve to stay quiet. Handle rattles gently—testing. Water drips under the threshold, inching toward your shoes.
Long silence. You crack the door—hallway empty. But every single window now shows your reflection—dozens of you—frozen in place, staring inward even as you move. You don't look at them. You bolt back to the stairwell, slam the bar; it finally gives.
You descend nineteen floors before legs give out completely. Lobby: two security guards waiting. They ask why you're crying, why you're soaked through. Shoes are dry—but your hands drip steadily. You have no memory of touching water. You never take an elevator again. You never work late again.

title: fifteen
instructions:
Camping solo deep in national forest backcountry—no trail markers, third night under razor-sharp stars. You wake at 3 a.m. to absolute, unnatural silence—no crickets, no wind, no distant owls. The forest feels like it's holding its breath with you. Then a single clear human whistle cuts the dark—high, melodic, far away.
It stops. Another answers—closer, different tone. A third joins, nearer still. Calling back and forth. Skin crawls; you know the old rule: never answer, never whistle back. But now the calls circle your campsite—patient, almost conversational.
You unzip the tent flap just enough to peek—moonlight bathes the clearing silver. Nothing moves. Whistling cuts off instantly. Dead quiet returns. Then a twig snaps directly behind the tent—sharp, deliberate. Another snap, slower. Wet animal musk drifts in—fur, rot-sweet fruit. Tent wall bows inward—something massive leaning against it, inches from your face.
You stop breathing entirely. Pressure releases; footsteps pad away into the trees. You wait frozen for hours. Finally crawl out shaking. Around the perimeter: perfect rings of footprints—human-shaped but too wide, too deep, long claw marks at each toe. They never crossed your guy lines—like respecting an invisible boundary. Or waiting for you to invite them in.
You pack in frantic silence, drop gear twice with trembling hands. Shoulder the pack, start down the trail by weak headlamp. Whistles resume immediately—behind you, ahead, flanking both sides. Not chasing—escorting. You walk faster; they match pace perfectly. Dawn still hours off. Trees stay empty when you look back, but the sound never leaves you.
It follows all the way to the trailhead. You lock yourself in the car, sob with raw relief, drive out without stopping. Months later you still jolt awake at 3 a.m. to perfect silence—and sometimes, just sometimes, a single curious whistle drifts in from far off, wondering if this time you'll finally answer.

title: sixteen
instructions:
Driving home after midnight on a narrow rural road, thick fog swallowing everything beyond the headlights. Radio off—only the low hum of tires and occasional gravel rattle. Red taillights glow ahead: a car pulled over, driver's door wide open, no one visible inside, dome light spilling weak yellow onto empty seats.
You ease past, stomach knotting tight—good Samaritan instinct warring with every true-crime warning you've ever absorbed. A mile later your own engine stutters once, twice, then dies completely. You coast to the narrow shoulder, turn the key—nothing. Battery light glares. Pop the hood—steam hisses up, burnt-rubber smell sharp in the wet air. Phone: zero bars. Total dead zone.
Silence presses in—no wind, no crickets, just soft drip from overhanging trees. You step out, wave the phone uselessly for signal. Headlights suddenly flare behind you—the same stopped car, rolling forward slowly now, engine purring low. No silhouette behind the wheel. Steering wheel turns slightly left, right, as if testing on its own.
You freeze. It halts ten feet away, door still hanging open, dome light steady. You back away step by step; it creeps forward, matching you exactly. Panic snaps—you turn and bolt into the dark woods beside the road, branches whipping your face and arms, lungs burning with cold air.
Behind you the engine revs once—low, almost amused—then falls quiet. You run until legs collapse, gasping, look back: fog has erased the road entirely. But you can still hear it—faint, steady tick-tick-tick of a cooling engine somewhere in the white wall of mist. Waiting patiently for you to come back out.

title: seventeen
instructions:
1:14 a.m. in your fourth-floor walk-up—thin walls, thin carpet. Three soft taps sound on the ceiling directly above your bed. Upstairs neighbor is elderly, rarely moves after dark. You bang the headboard once—silence falls. Exactly ninety seconds later, taps return—slower, more measured.
You pull on a robe, climb the stairs, knock on her door—no answer. Ear to wood: no TV, no footsteps, no breathing at all. Back downstairs, taps resume immediately—in sets of five now. You strap on noise-canceling headphones, crank white noise—vibration still travels through the mattress like a pulse.
By 3:00 a.m. the rhythm changes: long pause, four quick knocks, pause, four more—like counting down to zero. You sit up fast, heart thudding. Taps stop the instant you do. Ceiling stain you never noticed before has spread—dark, irregular, vaguely hand-shaped.
Single slow knocks return—directly over your face. One… two… three… four… five. Silence. Heavy shift overhead—floorboards bending. Then soft scraping circles the stain, like nails dragging. You dial 911—static only, distant rhythmic tapping echoing your own ceiling in the background.
Operator voice cuts in and out—swallowed. Scraping stops. A single dark drop falls from the stain onto your pillow—warm, copper-scented. Stain grows clearer: full hand now, fingers pressing down from above. You scramble out, run hallway, slam and lock bedroom door.
Knocking follows—instantly—tracking you ceiling to ceiling toward the stairwell. Barefoot, you fly down four flights, burst into the street. Behind you every light in your apartment flares bright—then dies completely.
You spend the night on a bench blocks away, watching. At dawn the upstairs window opens. Neighbor leans out, waves cheerfully, calls down she slept like a baby all night. You never go back upstairs. You never sleep on your back again.

title: eighteen
instructions:
Red-eye flight, window seat row 23—cabin dim, most passengers asleep under thin blankets. Earlier turbulence left your nerves raw; you can't close your eyes. You stare at the black window, seeing only your pale reflection staring back.
Around 4:00 a.m. cabin time the seatbelt sign dings on with no announcement. Plane levels, lights stay low. A flight attendant walks the aisle slowly—head down, hands clasped behind, black dress shoes instead of sneakers, no name tag. She passes your row without a glance.
She stops at row 20, leans over a sleeping man, whispers. He jerks awake, wide-eyed. She whispers again. He nods slowly, unbuckles, stands, follows her toward the rear galley. Curtain closes. Minutes drag—no one returns.
She reappears alone, same measured pace, same clasped hands. Stops at row 21—same whisper, same result. Another passenger rises, follows. Pulse climbs sharp. You nudge the man beside you—“Did you see?” He stares straight ahead, jaw locked, eyes glassy.
She reaches your row. Leans over the woman in 23A, whispers. Woman wakes, nods mechanically, stands, follows. Now just you and aisle-seat man left awake. Attendant turns to him—whispers. He rises like a puppet, walks away.
She turns to you. Face smooth, expressionless. Eyes too dark—pupils swallowing everything. She leans close—breath cold, ozone-sharp. Whispers your full name. Blood freezes. You shake your head hard: “No.”
She tilts her head, patient. Whispers your childhood street address next. Reaches for your seatbelt clasp. You slap her hand away—she doesn't flinch. Behind her the galley curtain parts: every taken passenger stands silent in a line, staring at you—faces blank, eyes empty.
Plane drops suddenly—stomach-lurching freefall. Oxygen masks tumble down, swaying useless. No one reaches for them. Attendant smiles—small, polite. “Time to go,” she says, voice layered with many overlapping tones.
You unbuckle, shove past her into the aisle. Silent passengers part slowly, reluctantly. You sprint toward the cockpit—door hangs open. Empty. No pilots. Plane flies itself.
You turn. She's walking toward you, unhurried. Every remaining passenger stands now, watching. Cabin lights flicker. You realize no one else is screaming—because you're the only one left who hasn't said yes.

title: nineteen
instructions:
Home alone for the weekend—partner on a work trip. House feels too large, too empty. You lock every door, check every window out of habit more than fear. 11:30 p.m.—power flickers once, twice, holds. You shrug, keep scrolling.
11:47 p.m.—doorbell rings sharp. You freeze. No one visits this late unannounced. Peephole shows empty porch—light on, street quiet. You wait. Nothing. Ten minutes later it rings again—same empty view.
You text partner: someone's ringing the bell. Instant reply: check the camera. App opens—feed pure black. Timestamp moving, recording, but nothing visible. Another long ring.
You call non-emergency—hold music loops while you pace. Operator finally answers; you explain. She says stay inside, doors locked, car en route when available. You hang up.
Doorbell again—longer, insistent. Then knocking—not front door—kitchen window. Slow, deliberate knuckle on glass. You back away. Sound circles house: front, side windows, back patio slider—always just out of sight.
You flip every light—house blazes bright. Knocking stops. Silence stretches thin. You creep to front window, peek through blinds. Dewy lawn: single bare footprint—too large, toes splayed unnaturally wide. Another appears ten feet closer to the house as you watch. Then another.
You sprint upstairs, barricade bedroom door with dresser. Phone buzzes—partner calling. You answer, whispering frantic. They sound calm: “I'm on my way home. Stay put.” But voice too steady—no road noise, no car hum.
You ask where they are. “Almost there,” they say. Line clicks dead. Downstairs back slider unlocks—soft metallic snick. Wet footsteps start—slow, deliberate—climbing the stairs.
You press into the closet, bury under coats, hold breath. Bedroom door creaks open. Dresser scrapes across floor like it weighs nothing. Footsteps stop right outside closet slats. Slow, patient breathing inches away.
Partner's voice, gentle: “You can come out now. It's just me.” But partner is three hundred miles away. You don't move. Closet door opens anyway—slowly, on its own.

title: twenty
instructions:
Exploring abandoned hospital on town's edge—urban explorer videos made it look thrilling. Flashlight beam cuts through dust; mask over nose against mildew stink. You step through broken glass into old psych ward—long corridors, doors hanging off hinges, scattered yellowed patient charts like dead leaves.
You film for your channel, whispering commentary: “Closed in '89 after too many… incidents.” Push into room labeled “Treatment 3”—restraint bed still bolted down, leather straps cracked with age. Rustle behind you—spin—empty hallway. Nerves, you mutter. Old building settling.
Deeper in: hydrotherapy room—giant stained porcelain tubs, rusted chains dangling from ceiling. Flashlight catches something curled in the farthest tub—mannequin, old medical dummy, head lolled, black-painted eyes staring.
You laugh shakily, pan camera: “Classic jump-scare setup.” As you turn away it sits up—slow, joints creaking audibly. Stands. Head tilts. No strings. No person inside. You stumble back—“Not funny”—voice cracking.
You run. Hallways twist; doors you passed now closed and locked. Phone dies mid-recording—battery was full minutes ago. Flashlight flickers—you slap it; beam steadies. Wet barefoot footsteps slap tile behind you—close, gaining.
Sprint to stairwell you entered through—door chained shut now, shiny new padlock gleaming. Footsteps closer. You turn—mannequin stands at corridor end, closer than physics allows. Painted eyes catch your light, reflect it back.
You bolt opposite way, lungs burning, into old cafeteria—tables overturned, chairs scattered. Center of room: every mannequin from every ward standing in perfect circle, facing inward, waiting. You skid to stop.
They don't move—yet. But more wet footsteps echo from hallway—dozens now. You back into corner. Flashlight shakes across plastic faces. One by one heads turn toward you. Painted mouths open—black, lipless voids.
Single voice—many voices layered—whispers your exact channel username. “We've been watching.” Circle tightens slowly. Flashlight dies. Total darkness.
You scream until throat bleeds raw. No one hears. Outside your car waits untouched. Your phone—dropped in corridor—keeps recording long after battery should die. Final frame: dozens of mannequins staring straight down at lens. One reaches forward.
Feed cuts. Video uploads automatically. Seventeen million views before anyone realizes you never came home.
